title: Cat Yellowish Loose Stool: Causes, Urgency, and Next Steps
meta_desc: Is your cat passing yellow, loose stool? Learn about potential causes like diet or infection, when to see a vet, and how photos help triage.
content_html:
TL;DR: Yellowish loose stool in cats often indicates rapid digestion or fat malabsorption and requires monitoring. Seek immediate veterinary care if the stool persists for over 48 hours or is accompanied by vomiting and lethargy.

What does it mean if my cat has yellowish loose stool?

Seeing a change in your cat's bathroom habits can be worrying. Yellowish, loose stool often indicates that food is moving through the digestive tract too quickly or that there is an issue with fat malabsorption or bile production. While it isn't always a cause for panic, it is a sign that your cat's gastrointestinal system is out of balance, possibly due to a sudden diet change, parasites, or a bacterial infection.

When is yellow diarrhea in cats considered an emergency?

  • Monitor your cat closely and schedule a non-emergency appointment if they are still eating, drinking, and acting normally.
  • Seek veterinary care sooner if the loose stool is accompanied by frequent vomiting or extreme lethargy.
  • Contact a professional if the diarrhea persists for more than 24 to 48 hours to prevent dehydration.

How can taking a photo of my cat's stool help the vet?

  • A clear photo allows your vet to see the exact shade of yellow and the stool's consistency.
  • Visual evidence helps professionals differentiate between simple irritation and complex issues like liver or gallbladder dysfunction.
  • Objective data from a photo helps the triage team determine the urgency of your cat's condition.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

In cats, yellowish loose stool (diarrhea) may be associated with hyperbilirubinemia, which can develop within 7-16 weeks after infection with liver flukes. Chronic fluke infestation can be fatal in severely affected cats. Early clinical signs of fluke infection appear between 7 and 16 weeks of infection, although signs may resolve without treatment by 24 weeks. Liver fluke infection is associated with inflammation and distention of the large bile ducts with neutrophilic and eosinophilic infiltrates, adenomatous biliary hyperplasia, peribiliary inflammation, and progressive fibrosis evolving into biliary cirrhosis.

Chapter: Hepatology, Emergency, Internal Medicine

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 473)

faqs.question: Can I give my cat human medicine for diarrhea?
faqs.answer: No, you should never give human medications to cats without specific veterinary guidance, as many common over-the-counter options are toxic to felines.

faqs.question: Should I withhold food from my cat if they have loose stool?
faqs.answer: Generally, you should not fast a cat, as it can lead to liver issues. Instead, offer small amounts of a bland diet like boiled chicken breast with no seasoning and consult your vet.

faqs.question: What are common causes of yellow stool?
faqs.answer: Common causes include dietary indiscretion, food sensitivities, intestinal parasites (like coccidia), or underlying issues with the liver or pancreas.
